content.full_text:
FAIR PRACTICES COMMISSION

October 3, 1975

75-10-128

Ms. Penelope L. Niland
Coordinator, South Central Coastwatch
1414 School House Road
Santa Barbara, California 93108

Re: Request for Opinion
No. 75-183

Dear Ms. Niland:

Thank you for your letter of September 23, 1975,
requesting an opinion with regard to your obligations
under the Political Reform Act. Since your letter does
not raise any substantial question involving an interpre-
tation of the Act, your request for a formal opinion is
denied.

The Commission has adopted a regulation, 2 Cal.
Admin. Code § 18239 (a copy of which is enclosed),
which clarifies the definition of a lobbyist. Your
problem is addressed in subsection (e)(2) of this regula-
tion.

Turning to your second question, the Act does not
expressly prohibit a lobbyist from serving as the treasurer
for a candidate for the Montecito Water Board. The Act
does, however, prohibit a lobbyist from contributing,
acting as an agent or intermediary in the making of a
contribution, or arranging for the making of a contribu-
tion to a state candidate or an elected state officer.
Moreover, the Act also prohibits a lobbyist from making
a gift, acting as an agent or intermediary in the making
of a gift, or arranging for the making of a gift, to a
state candidate, an elected state officer, a legislative
official, or an agency official. If you adhere to
these restrictions, it is permissible to serve as the
treasurer for the candidate to the Montecito Water Board.
To aid you in making this decision, I am enclosing a copy
of the Act, which contains definitions of the terms
"state candidate", "elected state officer," "agency
official", etc.; and a copy of a regulation promulgated

Ms. Penelope L. Niland                    2                    October 3, 1975


by the Commission clarifying the definition of "state agency"
(2 Cal. Adm. Code, Section 1829).

     I hope the foregoing is helpful. If you have any
further questions, please contact Ted Prim, a member of
the Commission staff.

                                        Sincerely,

                                        [signature]
                                        Daniel H. Lowenstein
                                        Chairman, for the Commission

DHL:gp
Enc.

Request No. _______, N.M. 9-25 recd
. 19 received 9-25-75
.. ponse Due 10- 9-75

SOUTH CENTRAL COASTWATCH
1414 School House Road
Santa Barbara, CA 93108
September 23, 1975

Fair Political Practices Commission
P.O. Box 807
Sacramento, CA 95804

Dear People:

This is a request for a ruling on whether or not I am a lobbyist under the terms of Proposition 9.

Coastwatch has been involved in the implementation of Proposition 20, the Coastal Zone Conservation Act, since 1973. During most of the time the person who functioned as co-ordinator of the group also was the major public spokesperson. This person appears before both the South Central Regional Commission and the State Commission on permit matters and on planning matters. For the past year-and-a-half I have served as co-ordinator and have served without pay but have been reimbursed my expenses which average about $40 per month. We recently received a grant from the Fate Norris Coastal Preservation Council which will pay me a salary of $350 for four months. I will continue to be reimbursed for my expenses at the same rate as before. I spend about 25-20 hours per week on Coastwatch matters. The remainder of my time is spent completing a Master's thesis at the University of California, Santa Barbara. I anticipate that during the next three months I will have some contact with State Legislators about the Coastal Plan which is about to be presented to them. I do not know how much time this will involve.

Should you rule that I am a lobbyist under the terms of the Act, I also need to know whether I can serve as the Treasurer for a candidate for the Montecito Water Board. I do not appear before the Board on any matters. The person who is running and in whose campaign I would serve is a citizen who has been part of Coastwatch. She is not a member of any official body before which I appear.

I had understood that people who do what I do as volunteers are not required to register with the Commission. Since, beginning this month, this condition has changed, I am requesting this ruling. If you need any more facts before you can arrive at your decision, please let me know.

Sincerely yours,

Penelope L. Milard
Co-ordinator, South Central Coastwatch
(805) 969-2857

embedding.qa_text: October 3, 1975 Ms. Penelope L. Niland Coordinator, South Central Coastwatch 1414 School House Road Santa Barbara, California 93103 Re: Request for Opinion No. 75-183 Dear Ms. Niland: Thank you for your letter of September 23, 1975, requesting an opinion with regard to your obligations under the Political Reform Act. Since your letter does not raise any substantial question involving an interpretation of the Act, your request for a formal opinion is denied. The Commission has adopted a regulation, 2 Cal. Admin. Code § 18239 (a copy of which is enclosed), which clarifies the definition of a lobbyist. Your problem is addressed in Subsection (e)(2) of this regulation. Turning to your second question, the Act does not expressly prohibit a lobbyist from serving as the treasurer for a candidate for the Montecito Water Board. The Act does, however, prohibit a lobbyist from contributing, acting as an agent or intermediary in the making of a contribution, or arranging for the making of a contribution to a state candidate or an elected state officer. Moreover, the Act also prohibits a lobbyist from making a gift, acting as an agent or intermediary in the making of a gift, or arranging for the making of a gift, to a state candidate, an elected state officer, a legislative official, or an agency official. If you can adhere to these restrictions, it is permissible to serve as the treasurer for the candidate to the Montecito Water Board. To aid you in making this decision, I am enclosing a copy of the Act, which contains definitions of the terms "state candidate", "elected state officer," "agency official", etc.; and a copy of a regulation promulgated Ms. Penelope L. Niland October 3, 1975 by the Commission clarifying the definition of "state agency" (2 Cal. Adm. Code, Section 18249). I hope the foregoing is helpful. If you have any further questions, please contact Ted Prim, a member of the Commission staff. Sincerely, Daniel H. Lowenstein Chairman, for the Commission DHL:gp Enc. Coastwatch has been involved in the implementation of Proposition 20, the Coastal Zone Conservation Act, since 1973. For the past year-and-a-half I have been the co-ordinator and have served without pay but have been reimbursed my expenses which average about $40 per month. We recently received a grant from the Lake Merced Coastal Preservation Council which will pay me a salary of $350 for four months. I will continue to be reimbursed for my expenses at the same rate as before.
